content.text: What Document Loading Does

Document loading is the first step in the RAG indexing pipeline. Its job is to extract raw text from source files in whatever format they live in — PDFs, HTML pages, Word documents, database rows, spreadsheets — and normalise the output into a standard Document object. Every downstream step (chunking, embedding, storing) works exclusively with Document objects, so the loader is the only place where file-format-specific logic lives.

A Document has two fields: page_content (the extracted text) and metadata (a dict of arbitrary key-value pairs). The metadata is critical for filtering at query time: it carries the source file path, page number, creation date, access level, and any other attributes you want to filter on later.

attrs.alt: Document loading: seven source types feeding into loaders that produce normalised Document objects
attrs.title: Document Loading

content.text: Common LangChain Loaders

LangChain ships over 100 document loaders under langchain_community.document_loaders. The most frequently used are PyPDFLoader (one Document per page), BSHTMLLoader (strips HTML tags), Docx2txtLoader, CSVLoader (one Document per row with configurable column mapping), TextLoader, WebBaseLoader, and DirectoryLoader (recursively loads a folder). Every loader returns list[Document] with consistent field names.

# Common LangChain document loaders
# Every loader produces list[Document] with page_content + metadata.

from langchain_community.document_loaders import (
    PyPDFLoader,            # PDF → one Document per page
    BSHTMLLoader,           # HTML → strips tags, returns text
    Docx2txtLoader,         # .docx → plain text
    CSVLoader,              # CSV → one Document per row
    TextLoader,             # .txt / .md → one Document per file
    WebBaseLoader,          # URL → fetches and parses HTML
    DirectoryLoader,        # loads all files in a folder
)

# PDF: each page becomes its own Document
pdf_docs = PyPDFLoader("docs/handbook.pdf").load()
print(f"PDF: {len(pdf_docs)} pages")
print(pdf_docs[0].metadata)   # {"source": "docs/handbook.pdf", "page": 0}

# HTML
html_docs = BSHTMLLoader("docs/index.html").load()
print(f"HTML: {len(html_docs)} docs, {len(html_docs[0].page_content)} chars")

# DOCX
docx_docs = Docx2txtLoader("docs/contract.docx").load()
print(f"DOCX: {len(docx_docs)} docs")

# CSV: each row is a separate Document; specify which columns to use
csv_docs = CSVLoader(
    file_path="docs/products.csv",
    content_columns=["description"],      # only this column becomes page_content
    metadata_columns=["sku", "category"],  # these become metadata
).load()
print(f"CSV: {len(csv_docs)} rows")
print(csv_docs[0].metadata)   # {"sku": "ABC-123", "category": "Electronics"}

# Web
web_docs = WebBaseLoader("https://example.com/faq").load()
print(f"Web: {web_docs[0].metadata}")

# Directory: load all .txt and .md files recursively
dir_docs = DirectoryLoader(
    "docs/",
    glob="**/*.{txt,md}",
    loader_cls=TextLoader,
    show_progress=True,
).load()
print(f"Directory: {len(dir_docs)} docs")
Custom Loaders for Proprietary Sources

When a data source has no built-in loader (internal APIs, custom databases, proprietary file formats), subclass BaseLoader and implement load(). The only contract is that load() returns list[Document]. The SQLite loader below demonstrates the pattern: iterate rows, build a Document per row, and populate metadata with all non-content columns.

# Custom document loader for a proprietary data source
# Subclass BaseLoader and implement load() to return list[Document].

from langchain_core.document_loaders import BaseLoader
from langchain_core.documents import Document
import sqlite3
from datetime import datetime


class SQLiteLoader(BaseLoader):
    """
    Load rows from a SQLite table as Documents.
    Each row becomes one Document with the specified text column as page_content
    and all other columns as metadata.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        db_path: str,
        table: str,
        content_column: str,
        metadata_columns: list[str] | None = None,
    ):
        self.db_path = db_path
        self.table = table
        self.content_column = content_column
        self.metadata_columns = metadata_columns or []

    def load(self) -> list[Document]:
        conn = sqlite3.connect(self.db_path)
        conn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row
        cursor = conn.cursor()

        cols = [self.content_column] + self.metadata_columns
        cursor.execute(f"SELECT {', '.join(cols)} FROM {self.table}")

        docs = []
        for row in cursor.fetchall():
            content = str(row[self.content_column])
            metadata = {
                col: row[col]
                for col in self.metadata_columns
                if col in row.keys()
            }
            metadata["source"] = f"sqlite://{self.db_path}/{self.table}"
            metadata["loaded_at"] = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
            docs.append(Document(page_content=content, metadata=metadata))

        conn.close()
        return docs


# Usage
loader = SQLiteLoader(
    db_path="data/support.db",
    table="tickets",
    content_column="body",
    metadata_columns=["ticket_id", "status", "created_at"],
)
docs = loader.load()
print(f"Loaded {len(docs)} support tickets")
print(docs[0].metadata)
Metadata Enrichment at Load Time

The metadata dict that comes out of most loaders is minimal (source path, page number). Enriching it at load time — before chunking — means every chunk inherits the enriched metadata automatically. Useful computed fields include a content hash (for incremental re-indexing), word count (for length-based filtering), file extension (for format-specific retrieval), and indexed_at timestamp (for recency filtering).

# Metadata enrichment: add computed metadata at load time
# Rich metadata enables precise filtering at query time.

from pathlib import Path
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader, TextLoader
import hashlib
from datetime import datetime


def enrich_metadata(doc: Document, file_path: str) -> Document:
    """
    Add computed fields to document metadata:
    - content_hash: for deduplication and change detection
    - word_count: for length-based filtering
    - file_extension: for format-specific retrieval
    - indexed_at: timestamp for time-based filtering
    """
    p = Path(file_path)
    doc.metadata.update({
        "content_hash": hashlib.md5(doc.page_content.encode()).hexdigest(),
        "word_count": len(doc.page_content.split()),
        "file_extension": p.suffix.lower(),
        "file_size_bytes": p.stat().st_size if p.exists() else 0,
        "indexed_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
    })
    return doc


def load_and_enrich(file_path: str) -> list[Document]:
    """Load a file and enrich every resulting Document with computed metadata."""
    ext = Path(file_path).suffix.lower()
    if ext == ".pdf":
        raw_docs = PyPDFLoader(file_path).load()
    else:
        raw_docs = TextLoader(file_path, encoding="utf-8").load()

    return [enrich_metadata(doc, file_path) for doc in raw_docs]


docs = load_and_enrich("docs/policy.txt")
for doc in docs:
    print(doc.metadata)
# {"source": "docs/policy.txt", "content_hash": "a3f1...", "word_count": 842,
#  "file_extension": ".txt", "file_size_bytes": 4821, "indexed_at": "2024-..."}
Complex Formats with Unstructured

Standard loaders treat a document as flat text. For PDFs with mixed layouts, scanned pages, embedded tables, or PowerPoint slides, the Unstructured library provides richer extraction. mode="elements" preserves the document's structural hierarchy — titles, narrative text, tables, and list items become separate Documents with a category metadata field. This allows downstream code to treat headings differently from body text and to handle tables without merging them into prose.

# Unstructured: extract text from complex formats (scanned PDFs, presentations)
# pip install unstructured[pdf,pptx] pdfminer.six python-pptx

from langchain_community.document_loaders import UnstructuredFileLoader

# Unstructured handles: PDF (including scanned), PPTX, XLSX, EML, MSG, and more.
# mode="elements" preserves document structure (titles, list items, tables separately).
# mode="single" (default) concatenates everything into one Document.

loader = UnstructuredFileLoader(
    "docs/quarterly_report.pdf",
    mode="elements",   # one Document per structural element
    strategy="hi_res", # OCR for scanned pages (requires tesseract)
)
elements = loader.load()

# Each element has type metadata
for el in elements[:5]:
    print(el.metadata.get("category"), "|", el.page_content[:80])
# Title | Q3 2024 Quarterly Report
# NarrativeText | Revenue increased 18% year over year to $142.3M...
# Table | Revenue | $142.3M | +18% YoY

# For PPTX: each slide's content becomes a separate Document
from langchain_community.document_loaders import UnstructuredPowerPointLoader
slides = UnstructuredPowerPointLoader("docs/pitch_deck.pptx", mode="elements").load()
print(f"Loaded {len(slides)} slide elements")
Common Pitfalls
Encoding errors: TextLoader defaults to the system locale, which fails on non-UTF-8 files. Always pass encoding="utf-8" or use autodetect_encoding=True.
Scanned PDFs returning empty text: PyPDFLoader extracts embedded text only. If pages are images, use UnstructuredFileLoader with strategy="hi_res" and tesseract installed.
Headers and footers polluting chunks: page headers ("Confidential — Acme Corp — Page 3") repeat on every page and degrade embedding quality. Strip them with a post-load cleaning step before chunking.

Large files creating one massive Document: some loaders (TextLoader) return the entire file as a single Document. This is fine — the splitter handles it — but log the token count so you can catch accidentally huge files that will time out the embedding API.
Parallel Loading for Large Corpora

Loading thousands of files sequentially is a common bottleneck. Document loading is almost entirely I/O-bound (reading files from disk or network), so parallelising with a thread pool gives near-linear speedup up to the number of CPU cores. The pattern below maps each file to a thread, collects results, and handles per-file errors without aborting the entire batch.
Post-Load Cleaning

Raw extracted text contains noise that degrades chunk quality and embedding accuracy: repeated page headers, footer boilerplate, control characters from encoding errors, and excessive blank lines. Cleaning happens after loading and before chunking, so a single cleaning function applies uniformly to all source formats. The five-step cleaner below handles the most common noise patterns and drops documents that are too short to be useful after cleaning.

# Post-load cleaning: remove noise before chunking
# Headers, footers, boilerplate, and excessive whitespace degrade chunk quality.

import re
from langchain_core.documents import Document


def clean_document(doc: Document) -> Document:
    """
    Apply a pipeline of cleaning steps to page_content.
    Returns a new Document to avoid mutating the original.
    """
    text = doc.page_content

    # 1. Remove PDF page headers/footers (e.g., "Confidential — Page 3 of 12")
    text = re.sub(
        r"(?im)^(confidential|proprietary|page\s+\d+\s+of\s+\d+|©\s+\d{4}.{0,60})$",
        "",
        text,
    )

    # 2. Collapse 3+ consecutive newlines to 2
    text = re.sub(r"
{3,}", "

", text)

    # 3. Remove non-printable control characters (except newline and tab)
    text = re.sub(r"[^	
 -~ -￿]", "", text)

    # 4. Strip leading/trailing whitespace on each line
    lines = [line.strip() for line in text.splitlines()]
    text = "
".join(lines)

    # 5. Skip empty documents after cleaning
    if len(text.strip()) < 50:
        return None   # caller should filter out None values

    return Document(page_content=text, metadata=doc.metadata)


def clean_documents(docs: list[Document]) -> list[Document]:
    cleaned = [clean_document(d) for d in docs]
    return [d for d in cleaned if d is not None]


# Usage after loading
from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader

raw_docs = PyPDFLoader("docs/annual_report.pdf").load()
print(f"Before cleaning: {sum(len(d.page_content) for d in raw_docs)} chars")

cleaned_docs = clean_documents(raw_docs)
print(f"After cleaning:  {sum(len(d.page_content) for d in cleaned_docs)} chars")
print(f"Docs removed (too short after cleaning): {len(raw_docs) - len(cleaned_docs)}")
Summary

Document loaders normalise diverse source formats into Document(page_content, metadata) objects. Use built-in LangChain loaders for common formats, subclass BaseLoader for proprietary sources, and enrich metadata at load time so every chunk inherits filterable attributes. For scanned PDFs or structured documents (presentations, spreadsheets), use Unstructured with mode="elements" to preserve document structure. Always set encoding explicitly and strip repetitive page headers before chunking.
